metadata.title: PFS Surrogate Endpoint — The Endpoint That Sometimes Lies

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. PFS as a surrogate — the endpoint that sometimes lies.

A cancer drug wins FDA approval showing it delays tumor progression by 2.5 months on a CT scan. Three years later the same drug fails to improve survival. Patients lived just as long whether they took it or not. The endpoint that got the drug approved measured the wrong thing. This is the progression-free survival surrogate problem — and it happens more often than the public knows.

Research the PFS-to-overall-survival surrogate endpoint problem: correlation between PFS benefit and OS benefit across cancer types, high-profile cases where PFS did not translate to OS benefit, and FDA's current policy.

props.command: claude "Research the PFS-to-overall-survival surrogate endpoint problem in oncology: what is the Spearman correlation between PFS benefit and OS benefit across cancer types (citing Prasad and Mailankody 2017, Kim and Prasad 2015), list five high-profile cases where PFS benefit did not translate to OS benefit, explain when PFS is a valid surrogate (colorectal first-line) vs invalid (second-line lung), and what the FDA's current policy is on PFS as primary endpoint."

beats.narration_text: Python script building 2x2 matrix of PFS benefit versus OS translation with case studies.

props.code:
CASES = [
    {"drug": "Bevacizumab (breast)", "PFS_mo": 5.9, "OS_benefit": False,
     "FDA_approved": True,  "approval_withdrawn": True},
    {"drug": "Avastin (glioblastoma)", "PFS_mo": 4.0, "OS_benefit": False,
     "FDA_approved": True,  "approval_withdrawn": False},
    {"drug": "FOLFOX (colon, 1L)",   "PFS_mo": 2.1, "OS_benefit": True,
     "FDA_approved": True,  "approval_withdrawn": False},
    {"drug": "Sunitinib (GIST)",      "PFS_mo": 24.1,"OS_benefit": True,
     "FDA_approved": True,  "approval_withdrawn": False},
    {"drug": "Palbociclib (breast)",  "PFS_mo": 10.0,"OS_benefit": None,
     "FDA_approved": True,  "approval_withdrawn": False},
]
print(f"{'Drug':<28} {'PFS benefit':>11}  {'OS confirmed':>13}  {'Status'}")
print("-" * 70)
for c in CASES:
    os = "YES" if c["OS_benefit"] else ("PENDING" if c["OS_benefit"] is None else "NO <- FLAG")
    status = "WITHDRAWN" if c["approval_withdrawn"] else "APPROVED"
    print(f"{c['drug']:<28} {c['PFS_mo']:>10.1f}mo  {os:>13}  {status}")

beats.narration_text: Matrix output — PFS benefit versus OS translation cases with FDA approval status.
Add a column: for each case, was the PFS benefit detected in a trial with OS as co-primary or secondary endpoint, and did that OS endpoint eventually read out positively or negatively?

props.command: claude "Add a column: for each case, was the PFS benefit detected in a phase 3 trial with OS as a co-primary or secondary endpoint, and did that OS endpoint eventually read out positively or negatively?"

beats.narration_text: Revised matrix with OS co-primary readout column — positive, negative, or never tested.

PFS measures whether the tumor shrinks or stops growing. OS measures whether the patient lives longer. These are not the same question, and treating one as a proxy for the other requires a strong biological assumption — that controlling the tumor controls the disease. That assumption fails when resistance develops quickly, when the therapy has significant toxicity, or when post-progression therapies are effective enough to erase the difference.

Read Prasad and Mailankody 2017 in JAMA Internal Medicine — the study that measured how often cancer approvals based on PFS or response rate are later confirmed with OS benefit. Find the percentage that were confirmed, the percentage that failed to confirm, and the percentage that were never tested.
Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At Nik Bear Brown, nikbearbrown dot com.
